speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 6. November 7, 1995. We're speaking with Mrs. Aranka Siegal. Mrs. Siegal, you were discussing liberation and the realization that the SS had left Bergen-Belsen. And your sister and many others had run towards the kitchen. You heard gunshots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And I was sure that my sister wouldn't come back, but miraculously she came back. And she even brought some food. And what happened is that the person had started to build a little fire outside the barracks. They were carrying planks off the barracks and pulling it apart into splinters and building fires. And they started cooking some food that they brought back like potatoes and onions and whatever they could find in the kitchen. And then the men started coming in from the men's camp. As they came in, we realized that the men had looked actually worse than we did. You know, much fewer men had survived than the women. This is a known fact that more women survived than the men. And they looked actually worse than we did. And as they came in, they kept asking for-- they were calling names and asking for their wives, mothers, sisters. And-- Where are we from, they kept asking. And as I was looking down at them, [DRINKS] and they were getting all these negative answers. Some of them were getting news that the people they were asking for had died. I saw the men form a little group, like a little cluster, and they started to recite the kaddish, which is the prayer for the dead. And somehow, as I said before, I always thought I would go home. Our house would be there. Our mother would be there probably at the stove cooking dinner. I mean, I must have been really-- just somehow, I guess, I fixed my mind on it in order to keep me going or, you know, like hypnotize myself because that's how I saw the end of the war. But when I heard these men recite the kaddish, suddenly the words of the kaddish came back to me from my childhood as I heard it, I guess, when my grandfather died and other funerals. And I found the words. I found that I could recite the words, too. And I walked over to the little cluster of men and joined them in reciting the kaddish. And as I did that, for the very first time, I admitted to myself my mother is dead. If they are all saying the prayer for the dead, they're dead. They must have died. And instead of a big celebration, as the kids always think-- You know, when I talk about liberation, they say you must have been so happy. You must have been jumping for joy. I always hate to let them down, and tell them no, actually at the very end was one of my very worst times because that was the first time I admitted that there was no going back.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no home, and you know, because the house, even if it was there, it's not a home. And that my mother wouldn't be there, and she must have died. If all these other women died, then it's true Mother died. And it was my first realization, I guess, that I was an orphan. And then I started thinking of the two little ones. Could they have died, too, at age four and age six? Is it possible? So after all I've seen, all these people being shot on the side of the road, or the dead bodies-- When we got to Bergen-Belsen, those enormous heaps of dead-- dead bodies, it was difficult for me to visualize that my mother and my little sister and brother were also dead, and maybe my grandmother. And I was going through all of them and trying to remember them as I was saying kaddish. And that was a very, very, very bad part of the liberation. And where was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister seemed to have been always busy, trying to get some information, get some help. And she found me there because one of the soldiers who saw how I was crying-- after the man walked away, I was standing there by myself. One of the liberators came over to me, and he happened to be Jewish. And he put his arm around me, which only made me cry even worse, because that was the first, you know, sympathetic caress or caring that I had heard or experienced. And he asked me how old I was, and to him, I told the truth, and I said 14. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said something like, 'Oh, you poor little thing,' in Yiddish. And he said, we are going to film this. And they were taking out cameras. And he said, we are going to make the whole world see what happened here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw them aim the cameras on those big heaps of bodies, you know? They were filming all that and us. And he was searching his pockets to give me something. And he didn't have anything. I guess he gave everything away already, but he had a whistle on his neck. And he took the whistle off his neck, and he put it on my neck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just couldn't get over him hugging me and being so kind to me. I knew my clothes were full of lice and how dirty I was and everything. And he didn't seem to take notice of it as he spoke to me. And then another one of his buddies came over and tapped him on the shoulder. He said good bye, and he walked away, and they were taking these photographs. I guess they had movie cameras. And I went to stand by the heap of bodies. Well, my sister came with me at that point. And we were standing there thinking, if it's going to be in the newsreel, maybe somebody, like my father if he survived, would recognize us and know that we survived. We didn't know what we looked like. Nobody would have recognized us, but of course, we weren't aware. We never saw a mirror.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to stand there, actually, so I am sure I'm part of some kind of a newsreel because we were standing there. And after that, came the thing with the canned food, and my getting so sick, and they carried me off to a hospital. And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came with me. Oh, she came with me all the way. As a matter of fact, some German women were around the doctor to give us a bath. And they had us on these tables, and they were sponging us off with warm water. And I kept holding onto her hand even then. And I fell asleep, I found out later on, when the warm water and the sponge was on my body. I just fell asleep, and the next thing I knew, I was in the hospital. Was very, very sick for a very long time. I had to have blood transfusion. One of the Belgian doctors who was a volunteer gave me some blood. And then an Italian doctor also-- I think they were interns that had come to volunteer there. And they gave me a blood. As a matter of fact, later on, the Italian was-- Italian one was joking with me, the Italian doctor, and he said, well, you're part Christian, part Italian. You have some of my blood, you know. And, you know, little by little, I started to get off the bed and walk and so forth. And then the Swedish Red Cross came while I was still crying, again, to go home, by the way. They come to-- came to interview us, and they said that all the orphans they were going to take to Sweden, you know. And I was still crying, no, I don't want to go to Sweden. I want to go home. And then they-- of course my sister just signed up for both of us that we going to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then after liberation, you were in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in a quarantine. In Sweden, they put us in a quarantine because they didn't know what diseases we might have, you know. I mean we came there from a horrible place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. I was in a quarantine, and from the quarantine, I went to a makeshift school. And from the makeshift school, we went to work in two different factories. One was a match factory, very famous big match factory in Sweden that's no longer there. And then we went to Husqvarna, another. They make all the motorcycles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sweden? By boat. They took us first by train from the hospital. We went by train to Lubeck, which is a port. And then they put us on boats, and they took us over to Malmo in Sweden. And then in Malmo, they had these white buses that they put us all in, and they took us to the quarantine. And they gave us medical help and you know, try to bring us back to being human again. Because a lot of them really were just so far gone, you know. It was just terrible. There was a lot of mental patients. There was a psychiatric ward, and really, we were lucky that we maintained our sanity. And I'm sure that has a lot to do with having each other because I found out during the war by talking to other survivors that most of the ones who survived, survived because they had somebody with them. Either a mother or sister, cousin, good friend or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody they loved.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Yup. To survive by yourself an extreme situation and without the feeling that you belong, having a strong feeling, strong sense of belonging or religion, it's impossible to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, of course, the most important thing in my life is teaching the Holocaust. As a matter of fact, even writing, which had become so important, and of course all my writing has to do with the Holocaust because they are autobiographical. And both books are about my experiences, one before the war and the other one looking back at the experiences and coming to terms with it after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come in your books there isn't a vivid description of your experiences in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camps? Well, very tough topic to write about, number one. Number two, by the time I wrote my books 15 years ago, there were so many Holocaust books out, you know? I felt that they already know, meaning the world already knows what it was like in the Holocaust within the documentaries and all the books that had been published prior to mine. But I would really like to write about, I decided, and write for young people, is what it was like before the war, who we were, what we were like, what we were thinking. In-- in other words, I wanted to communicate the fact that we were just like the children over here. We weren't that different because, you know, once the people meet us in those books about the camps, dehumanized and everything, they can't empathize with us. They can't possibly relate to people like that. But if they met me the way I was before, you know, a child like themselves, going to school and looking ahead to the future and planning-- like for instance, I always wanted to write, but I never thought, you know, that I would get a chance because especially when my education stopped after the fifth grade, I thought that would be, you know, the thing that I would want to do. I would want to write, but it just didn't seem that it was ever going to be. And now even though my writing became so important to me after I raised my children. Of course, I went to college--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Sweden for three and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- when did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met now my husband a year after I came out here. I was 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you-- and his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name is Gilbert Siegal, and he is the greatest man that ever lived, only second to my father. Without him, I would have never healed, never. He helped me heal. He helped me go to school. He helped me become a writer. He helped me get my education, and he gave me total support in everything I wanted to do. And he was just my father, my brother, and my husband, and best friend still.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married when I was 21, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met by my looking for a job. And I went to see his father about the job. I was modeling at the time. Not being able to speak the English language, somebody advised me that I go to Mademoiselle Modeling School and try to work as a model. And sure enough, I got a job in the garment section. But then a man from Connecticut, which is where I went first went when I came to this country. I went to Bridgeport, Connecticut, to live with my mother's sister. And we knew this man, Mr. Shapiro, there who I worked for in Bridgeport. And he said, when you go to New York-- because I said I want to go to New York. I want to go to college, and I want to work in the city. He said, well, when you go, you have to go see this man Jack Siegal, and he'll probably give you work because he's a very nice man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He said you are too short to model for me because he was a fur buyer, and he said you can't model furs because you're too short. But I have this son who is a lawyer, and he knows a lot of people, and maybe he could help you get a job. And he sent me to meet his son, Gilbert. And what he really had a mind-- but he didn't tell us-- is that he wanted the two of us to meet because he liked me very much. And he felt Gilbert was old enough to get married because, he was at that time, pushing 30. And he thought that this little girl might be just the right wife for him. So we met and didn't really hit it off right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when were you ultimately married?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1949, and we got married in '51. So we dated for a long time, and I went to school at night and modeled during the day. And then we got married, February of 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to this country, did people ask you about your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never spoke of it. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: You tried-- you tried to forget?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I also didn't want to be treated differently, you know. Because to be pitied is not a very good feeling. And I didn't want to talk about it because some people might have thought that I was trying to draw on sympathy. And also, it's a very tough thing to talk about. As you can see, I still can't talk about my mother without crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have two lovely children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we had a daughter, and her name is Rise. And she's named for my mother. And she lives in Europe now because she's married. And-- here I came to the United States, but when she got married, she married a European man. And she lives in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son, Joseph who is three years younger lives in California, and he's in computers, by the way, and she is in investments.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were raising your children, did your Holocaust experience affect the way you raised them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure. I'm sure subconsciously even, but I consciously tried to raise them as American as apple pie. I became very American myself, forced myself, having married an American man. I wanted to model myself after his mother who was American, you know. Even though-- I mean, my cooking and a lot of inbred things was my mother, you know, but she was my model as far as how an American wife and mother behaves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in-- once you were in this country, all these past years, did you have dreams--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh!

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not dreams, my dear. Nightmares. I still get nightmares to this day. I had nightmares just two nights ago. And I still get them, but, you know, they're less frequent. They're not as violent, and they don't unsettle me for as long a time afterwards. But yes, of course. This is something, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: I have somebody say to me not too long ago-- and this really upset me because it's a very good friend, and he's a very lovely person. He's a Mormon. And I have spoken at his church several times, and they're very good friends. And one time-- not me, but somebody else had brought up the Holocaust while will having dinner at a different friend's house-- and he said, oh, can't we just let that go, for God's sakes? It's almost 40 years ago-- at the time. And it just really upset me, but I didn't say anything. But the host who had brought up the subject to start with was a Christian but a photographer. And he had gone to Israel to do some shooting there. He said, how can you say that? Don't you realize that Aranka is sitting here and what happened to her? And he says, I know, but a lot of things happen to a lot of people, and we ought to just go on and forget the past.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, it's easy for people to say it who are not part of the past. But to me the past is the present or part of the present. Though I am a very positive person, and I try to live in the future-- really I'm a very up person-- but the past is with me every day of my life. And it will be till the end of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my bubbe, my mother's mother. And her name was Feiga Rosner. She is the one that was resisting to leave the farm, but she was taken on the last, in the spring of 1944, and marched straight to the gas chambers that my sister Roszi, who was staying with her at the time, and helped her walk because she was too sick to walk when she got off the cattle car. This is my sister Lilli [? Louisa, ?] and she was married. So her name was Katz Hollander.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph was taken in our city when she was about 19, 20 years old. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my father, Ignac Davidovitz. And he was about 20 years old here at the most. So this was taken when he was a very young man at home in our city. This is my husband, Gilbert, on the right, in his Air Force uniform. And he was about 22 years old. But I wanted this picture of him. And on the left is a picture of me when I was 40 years old. It's my 40th birthday. [PAUSES FOR 24 SECONDS] This is wedding picture of my daughter, Rise, and her husband, Olivier. And this was taken 11 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a photo taken at my daughter's wedding, again, but she is with her brother. So that's Rise and Joseph. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is my sister, Violet. We survived together, and this picture was taken about 10 years ago. Her name now is Violet Sloan. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my husband, Gilbert, at his high school graduation. And on the right is a picture of me-- it's my passport picture, actually, coming over here-- around the same age. We're both 18. This is our house in Beregszasc 50 years later in its very dilapidated state, that this friend had taken just recently and bought it back for me. These are my two books, my two autobiographical books. And the first one, has a family picture on it, the only picture I have of my family. This is Lajos, my oldest sister's husband. And-- the woman next to him-- the man is Lajos Katz, my sister's husband. And the woman next to him is my sister Lilli. And on Lilli's left is Roszi. I am the little girl with her hand over her eyes. And the little girl on the bottom is my sister Lilli's daughter, Manci, and my little brother Sandorn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other book?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other book, is the sequel to the first one. And that's my passport picture on the cover.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the award medallion?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first book has received several awards, and the most prestigious is the Newbery Honor Award; therefore, each of the books will carry the seal. This is the Anne Frank documentary I had mentioned earlier that was made for the 25th anniversary of Anne Frank, where I continue Anne Frank's story from the time she at arrived in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is my husband, Gilbert Siegal, the man I have been married to since I was 21 years old, but I've actually known him since I was 18.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the most important part of us, I think, is our children. One is 3,000 miles to the east in Paris. The other 3,000 miles to the west in California, but Rise faxed a thought she wanted included. And that is, of the many childhood memories that she has of growing up as Aranka's daughter, there was a Christmas morning, when after a Christmas party, every guest had received a handmade card with words from 'A Christmas Carol' that would relate to that individual. And the card they made for our Aranka was a sketch of her and the words, 'joy to the world.' And she said in this fax to be heard today, that it struck her then how perfectly the sentiment fit her mother. And how often she thought about that as she grew up. After the horror, the suffering, the loss, the unspeakable, this courageous little girl was able to find joy in her life and is always bringing joy to others. And she ends with saying to you who are making this film, please do not end a video taping of her tragic memories without recording one of her joyous smiles. And from the other direction, from California, the son was unable to keep his remarks short. But what he did finally wind up saying, I think, in essence-- he remarked on the-- the apathy that has seeped over the world, in the 50 years since the Holocaust. And he says that his mother, Aranka, is a survivor of that apathy. And she survives a second time. That despite all that she had suffered, she continues to drive and fly to those who need to hear her, including those whose aim it is to tell others. And to tell that the worst that people can do when hate takes over from the most compelling viewpoint, that of a survivor, to end such hatred. And he said he hopes that in addition to her books that grew out of her experience, and her ability to talk about them, will grow-- help future generations to-- to know and realize the horror of unchecked prejudice. And I'm very glad that they had a chance to be heard. For me, there would only be just admiration for her, and the realization that this wonderful woman personifies everything that she writes about of her own mother and her grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Is that it? Oh, you're still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. [LAUGHTER]
